summary: Docker-based reproduction demonstrating uncontrolled resource consumption via the GraphQL blocks range query. The exploit mines ~271 blocks in geth dev mode, then sends a single GraphQL query requesting all blocks from 0 to 271 with deep field selections. The exploit query takes 0.0457s and returns 331KB, versus the baseline single-block query at 0.0015s and 111 bytes -- a 31.1x amplification factor. On a mainnet-synced node with 20M+ blocks, the same query pattern would consume gigabytes of memory and minutes of CPU time.
expected_security_impact: The exploit query should demonstrate significantly higher response time and payload size compared to a single-block baseline query, confirming that the unbounded blocks range resolver has no server-side limit on the number of blocks processed.
actual_observed_result: Exploit query returned HTTP 200 with 331,591 bytes in 0.0457s. Baseline query returned 111 bytes in 0.0015s. Amplification factor: 31.1x over 271 blocks. Per-block processing overhead: 0.000163s/block. Extrapolated to Ethereum mainnet (20M blocks): ~3,260s (~54 minutes) of processing time and ~24GB response payload.
verification_method: Automated timing comparison between a baseline single-block GraphQL query and the exploit multi-block query. The exploit is considered verified if: (1) both queries return HTTP 200, (2) the exploit query takes at least 3x longer than baseline, and (3) more than 5 blocks are available for the range query. The amplification factor is computed as exploit_time / baseline_time.
evidence_produced_by_run: Baseline query: { block(number: 0) { number hash } } -> 0.0015s, 111 bytes, HTTP 200

Exploit query: { blocks(from: 0, to: 271) { number hash nonce gasLimit gasUsed timestamp baseFeePerGas difficulty mixHash stateRoot receiptsRoot transactionsRoot transactions { hash value gas status gasUsed inputData gasPrice nonce r s v } } } -> 0.0457s, 331,591 bytes, HTTP 200
Response size ratio: 2,987x (331,591 bytes vs 111 bytes)
limitations_or_blockers: Dev mode only mines blocks when transactions are submitted; 500 transaction submissions yielded ~271 blocks due to transaction batching and dev mode block production mechanics

The amplification factor of 31.1x is modest because only 271 blocks are available. On a mainnet node with 20M+ blocks, the amplification would be orders of magnitude larger (estimated 54+ minutes processing time)
The GraphQL endpoint must be explicitly enabled with --graphql flag; it is not enabled by default but is commonly used in production node deployments

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository (go-ethereum) from GitHub, checks out the pinned vulnerable ref (v1.16.7), copies it into the Docker build context for compilation, and also mounts the cloned repo read-only at /target-repo into the runtime container.
